content.text: When you run a large language model (LLM) or any AI model locally on your own computer, rather than on a cloud provider's servers, you are asking your machine to perform operations that are simultaneously memory-intensive and computationally demanding.

refer to the minimum and recommended specifications your machine must satisfy so that a given model can load into memory, perform inference (generate tokens), and respond within a timeframe that is actually useful. These requirements span four key categories: GPU video memory (VRAM), system RAM, storage speed, and CPU capability. Understanding them before selecting a model can be the difference between a productive workflow and a machine that either refuses to load the model at all or produces responses at a rate of one word per minute.
The requirements differ fundamentally from conventional software requirements because AI models are not programs that execute instructions — they are

, billions of floating-point numbers that must reside in fast, accessible memory during every single forward pass. A 7-billion-parameter model quantized to 4 bits occupies roughly 4 to 5 GB of VRAM. A 70-billion-parameter model at the same quantization needs 35 to 40 GB. These numbers directly dictate which GPU or GPUs you need, and they scale non-linearly with context length because of the key-value (KV) cache that stores attention state for every token currently in a conversation.

The landscape of consumer hardware changed significantly between 2023 and 2025. GPUs like the NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM) and the RTX 3090 (also 24 GB) made it practical for individuals to run 7B to 34B models at conversational speeds. Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture, with M2 Ultra and M3 Ultra offering up to 192 GB shared between CPU and GPU, further expanded the accessible tier of hardware. Quantization techniques, particularly the GGUF format's Q4_K_M and Q5_K_M variants used by llama.cpp and Ollama, compressed capable models to a fraction of their original size without catastrophic quality degradation. This combination of hardware and quantization is why local AI inference became viable for everyday developers and researchers, not just those with access to data center hardware.

Mismatched hardware is the single most common reason developers abandon local LLM setups. Downloading a 40 GB model file and then discovering your GPU has only 8 GB of VRAM, and that CPU-only inference will produce responses at 2 tokens per second, wastes hours and creates genuine frustration. Hardware requirements are the first filter to apply before selecting a model, a runtime, or a quantization level. Getting this wrong has real costs: wasted download time, wasted electricity, and occasionally GPU memory overflows that crash the runtime mid-response and corrupt the output.

Beyond the question of whether a model runs at all, hardware determines the quality of your experience and your outputs. A model that barely fits in VRAM has no headroom for a longer KV cache, capping your effective context window. A model partially offloaded to system RAM runs 5 to 20 times slower than a fully GPU-resident model, making it impractical for interactive chat or real-time agentic workflows. Understanding hardware requirements allows you to make principled decisions: run a smaller model that fits entirely in GPU memory for fast iteration, versus a larger model with CPU layer offloading for batch workloads where latency matters less than output quality.

Privacy and cost are the other central motivations for local inference. When a model runs on your own machine, your prompts and completions never leave that machine. There are no API keys to manage, no per-token billing to watch, and no data retention or logging policies to worry about. But this privacy advantage only exists in practice if your hardware can run inference at a speed that makes the local option genuinely usable. A machine that takes 10 minutes to generate a response, because the model is CPU-offloaded and running over system RAM, is technically local but practically unusable for most workflows.
Key Benefits of Understanding Hardware Requirements

Knowing your hardware profile upfront lets you filter the model landscape to candidates that will actually run well. You skip the multi-hour cycle of downloading large checkpoint files only to discover they will not load on your available VRAM.

Hardware requirements tell you whether you need to purchase a new GPU or whether your existing machine can run a useful model with quantization applied. This prevents over-investment in hardware whose incremental benefit does not match the incremental cost for your specific use case.

VRAM capacity and memory bandwidth directly predict tokens per second for a given model size. Once you understand the underlying relationship, you can estimate inference speed before committing to a hardware purchase or a model download.

Local inference keeps prompts on your hardware. Understanding the requirements ensures you can actually run the models you need for sensitive domains, including medical records, legal documents, proprietary source code, and personal correspondence.

Local models run without internet access. For regulated industries, remote field work, or air-gapped research networks, this is a hard requirement, and hardware requirements determine which quality tier of model you can achieve in that offline context.

No per-token API costs means you can iterate rapidly, run long prompts, generate many alternative completions, and explore adversarial inputs without watching a billing meter. This fundamentally changes the economics and pace of AI feature development.
VRAM is fixed at purchase time:

GPU video memory cannot be expanded after purchase. Unlike system RAM, VRAM is soldered to the GPU die. If you buy a 12 GB card and later need 16 GB for a model you want to run, there is no upgrade path short of replacing the entire GPU.
Consumer VRAM capacity has been stagnant:

As of mid-2025, the highest-VRAM consumer GPU remains the RTX 4090 at 24 GB. Models, however, keep growing. Running a 70B model locally requires either multiple consumer GPUs, Apple Silicon with large unified memory, or enterprise-class hardware — all of which carry significant cost.
CPU inference is a fallback, not a practical solution:

Offloading model layers to system RAM and computing them on the CPU is possible via llama.cpp, but CPU memory bandwidth is 5 to 30 times lower than GPU VRAM bandwidth. A model that runs at 60 tokens per second on a GPU may run at 2 to 4 tokens per second when fully CPU-offloaded, which is insufficient for interactive workflows.

Q4 quantization compresses a model to roughly 4.5 bits per weight on average, which enables large models to fit in small VRAM but does introduce quality loss. The degradation is modest for general instruction following (typically under 1% on standard benchmarks), but can be more noticeable on tasks requiring precise numerical reasoning or retrieval of rare factual knowledge.

A GPU running LLM inference at full load draws 300 to 450 watts continuously. Running an inference server for many hours per day adds measurably to electricity costs and requires adequate case cooling to prevent thermal throttling or long-term hardware wear.

CUDA on NVIDIA, ROCm on AMD, and Metal on Apple Silicon have different capability levels and different support across inference runtimes. Flash Attention and tensor parallelism optimizations may be available on some GPU families but not others, creating a fragmented compatibility matrix that requires research before deployment.
Every local inference session has a memory budget divided into two components: the

. The weights are the trained parameters of the neural network, fixed after training, and they must be resident in VRAM before any inference can begin. The KV cache stores the attention key and value tensors computed for each token in the current conversation — it grows with every token, both in the input prompt and in the generated response.
The model weight memory requirement is estimated by the following formula:
is the total number of parameters (e.g. 7 billion = 7 × 10⁹),

is the quantization bit-width (4 for Q4, 8 for Q8, 16 for FP16, 32 for FP32), and the 1.15 factor accounts for runtime overhead including activation buffers and temporary tensors. Dividing by 8 converts bits to bytes. Dividing the result by 1024³ converts to gigabytes.
The KV cache occupies additional VRAM that grows proportionally with context length:

attrs.latex: \text{VRAM}_{\text{KV}} = \frac{2 \times n_{\text{layers}} \times n_{\text{KV heads}} \times d_{\text{head}} \times L_{\text{ctx}} \times 2}{1024^3} \text{ GB}

content.text: is the number of transformer layers,
is the number of key-value attention heads (which is often smaller than query heads in models using grouped-query attention),
is the dimension per attention head,

is the context length in tokens, and the first 2 accounts for both keys and values while the trailing 2 is the byte size of FP16 precision. For Llama 3.1 8B at a 32,768-token context, the KV cache adds approximately 4 to 6 GB to the weight footprint, which is significant for GPUs in the 8 to 12 GB VRAM range.
Memory Bandwidth as the Speed Bottleneck

During autoregressive decoding, the GPU generates exactly one token per forward pass. Each forward pass requires reading the entire weight matrix from VRAM into the GPU's compute units. This makes LLM inference fundamentally
, not compute-bound. A GPU with twice the TFLOPS but the same memory bandwidth will generate tokens at nearly the same speed. The practical rule of thumb for decode throughput is:

attrs.latex: \text{tokens per second} \approx \frac{\text{Memory Bandwidth (GB/s)}}{\text{Model Size in VRAM (GB)}}

content.text: An RTX 4090 provides 1008 GB/s of memory bandwidth. A 7B model in Q4_K_M occupies roughly 4.5 GB of VRAM. The estimated throughput is

theoretical passes per second. Real-world performance lands at 65 to 95 tokens per second due to kernel launch overhead, attention computation, and memory access patterns. An NVIDIA A100 80GB with 2000 GB/s bandwidth running a 13B FP16 model at 26 GB yields approximately

tokens per second, consistent with observed benchmarks. These estimates are surprisingly predictive and explain why bandwidth-per-dollar is the primary metric for evaluating hardware specifically for LLM inference.

The GGUF quantization format used by llama.cpp and Ollama stores weights in variable-precision blocks. Q4_K_M means 4-bit quantization using the K-quant method, which reserves 6-bit precision for the most sensitive weight tensors, combined with a mixed-block strategy. The result is approximately 4.5 bits per weight on average. Perplexity (a measure of language model quality) degrades by roughly 0.1 to 0.5 perplexity points versus FP16, a difference that is typically imperceptible in conversational use. Q5_K_M at 5.5 bits per weight closes most of the remaining gap for tasks that are more quality-sensitive. Q2_K at 2.5 bits per weight achieves the smallest file size but shows noticeable degradation on knowledge-heavy tasks.
Architecture — Hardware Stack and Data Flow

The diagram below shows the four hardware components of a local inference setup and how they interact during a single inference session. It also illustrates quantization tradeoffs and representative inference speeds across hardware tiers.

attrs.alt: Local LLM hardware architecture. NVMe SSD holds model weights, which load into system RAM and then transfer over PCIe DMA to GPU VRAM, where tensor cores run the transformer forward pass. CPU handles tokenization and orchestration. Side panels show quantization levels and VRAM estimates per model size. Bar chart at bottom shows inference speed across hardware tiers.

content.text: The data flow during a single inference call follows these steps. First, at model startup, the quantized GGUF file is memory-mapped or loaded from NVMe SSD into system RAM. If a discrete GPU is available, the weight tensors are transferred over the PCIe bus (DMA) into GPU VRAM. The CPU tokenizes the user's input string into integer token IDs. The GPU then runs the forward pass across all transformer layers, computing attention scores and feed-forward layer activations while reading weights directly from VRAM. The output logit tensor, representing a probability distribution over the vocabulary, is transferred back to CPU for sampling. The selected token ID is appended to the sequence and the decode loop repeats, one token per iteration, until an end-of-sequence token is produced or the maximum generation length is reached.

The most important architectural constraint is that GPU VRAM must hold the entire active weight matrix at all times during inference, not stream it from disk. If the model is larger than available VRAM, individual transformer layers must be swapped between VRAM and system RAM via the PCIe bus for each forward pass. PCIe 4.0 x16 provides roughly 16 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth, compared to approximately 1000 GB/s for GDDR6X on a consumer GPU. This roughly 60-fold bandwidth gap is why CPU layer offloading so severely degrades throughput, even when the CPU itself is fast.
When to Run Models Locally and When Not To

Legal documents, medical records, personal journals, and proprietary source code that cannot leave a machine require local inference. There is no API-based alternative when the data handling policy prohibits external transmission.

Classifying large document collections, generating structured extractions across thousands of records, or embedding an entire corpus for a retrieval system — workloads where per-token billing would accumulate to significant cost.
Testing system prompts, few-shot configurations, output format constraints, and prompt injection defenses without incurring API costs across hundreds of test calls.
Aircraft, maritime platforms, field research locations with unreliable connectivity, and regulated networks that prohibit outbound API traffic.

Watching memory consumption, measuring latency, switching between quantization levels, and observing generation parameter effects teaches you how LLMs behave at the hardware level in ways that cloud APIs cannot expose.
Scenarios Where Cloud Inference Is Better

GPT-4, Claude 3.7, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and similar models operate at parameter counts and training scales that no consumer hardware can run locally. If your task needs that capability tier, a local model will produce lower-quality outputs.

A machine with only 8 GB of VRAM or no discrete GPU at all will struggle to run models useful for production tasks. The cloud offers immediate access to powerful inference without hardware investment.

A local model server consumes VRAM and electricity even at idle. For occasional, low-volume use, API costs may be lower than the opportunity cost of dedicating a machine to an always-on inference process.
Latency is critical for short inputs:
A cloud API's network round-trip plus inference time can be under 500ms for short prompts, which is often faster than a locally hosted model's cold-start plus decode time on modest hardware.
How to Implement — Setting Up Local Inference
The first step before any model selection is a hardware audit. Run these commands to establish your baseline VRAM, system RAM, and storage speed:

# On Linux with an NVIDIA GPU
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=name,memory.total,memory.free --format=csv,noheader
# Output example:
# NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090, 24576 MiB, 24100 MiB

# On macOS (Apple Silicon)
system_profiler SPHardwareDataType | grep Memory
# Output example:
#   Memory: 64 GB

# System RAM on Linux
free -h

# Identify storage type (ROTA=0 means flash/SSD, ROTA=1 means spinning HDD)
lsblk -d -o NAME,ROTA,SIZE,TYPE

# Rough sequential read speed test for the drive holding your models
sudo hdparm -t /dev/nvme0n1
# Good NVMe SSD: 3,000-7,000 MB/s
# SATA SSD:      400-600 MB/s
# HDD:           80-200 MB/s
Ollama is the recommended starting point. It handles model download, GGUF format management, GPU detection, server startup, and API exposure with a single command:

# macOS or Linux
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

# Windows: download the installer from https://ollama.com/download

# Verify installation
ollama --version

# Pull a model — Ollama selects the quantization level based on your VRAM
ollama pull llama3.1:8b          # ~4.7 GB download; needs 6+ GB VRAM
ollama pull phi3:mini            # ~2.3 GB; fits in 4 GB VRAM
ollama pull mistral:7b-instruct  # ~4.1 GB; strong instruction following

# Start interactive chat
ollama run llama3.1:8b

# List downloaded models with their sizes
ollama list

# After starting a model, verify GPU utilization in a second terminal
nvidia-smi
# Healthy output: VRAM used = roughly the model size
# Unhealthy: VRAM used < 500 MB -> model fell back to CPU; check GPU drivers
Step 3: Call the API from Python
Ollama exposes an OpenAI-compatible REST API on port 11434. Any application that already uses the OpenAI Python SDK can point to the local server with a single parameter change:

# pip install openai
from openai import OpenAI

# Direct the client to the local Ollama server instead of api.openai.com
client = OpenAI(
    base_url='http://localhost:11434/v1',
    api_key='ollama',   # Required by the SDK; Ollama does not validate it
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='llama3.1:8b',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are a helpful assistant.'},
        {'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain memory bandwidth in one sentence.'},
    ],
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=200,
)

# Output arrives from your local GPU
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
This script estimates VRAM requirements for any model size and quantization level before downloading the file, allowing you to verify fit before committing to a large download:

# vram_calculator.py
# Estimate VRAM needed to run a given LLM locally.
# Usage: python vram_calculator.py

def estimate_vram(
    params_billions,
    quant_bits,
    context_length=4096,
    n_layers=32,
    n_kv_heads=8,
    head_dim=128,
    overhead_factor=1.15,
):
    # Model weight VRAM: params * bytes_per_param * overhead
    params = params_billions * 1e9
    weight_bytes = params * (quant_bits / 8.0) * overhead_factor
    weight_gb = weight_bytes / (1024 ** 3)

    # KV cache VRAM: 2 (K and V) * layers * kv_heads * head_dim * ctx_len * 2 bytes (FP16)
    kv_bytes = 2 * n_layers * n_kv_heads * head_dim * context_length * 2
    kv_gb = kv_bytes / (1024 ** 3)

    return {
        'weights_gb': round(weight_gb, 2),
        'kv_cache_gb': round(kv_gb, 2),
        'total_gb': round(weight_gb + kv_gb, 2),
    }


CONFIGS = [
    # (label,                  params_b, bits, ctx,    layers, kv_h, h_dim)
    ('Phi-3 mini  Q4_K_M',    3.8,      4,    4096,   32,     8,    96),
    ('Llama 3.1 8B Q4_K_M',   8.0,      4,    4096,   32,     8,    128),
    ('Llama 3.1 8B Q4 32K',   8.0,      4,    32768,  32,     8,    128),
    ('Llama 3.1 8B FP16',     8.0,      16,   4096,   32,     8,    128),
    ('Mistral 7B Q5_K_M',     7.0,      5,    8192,   32,     8,    128),
    ('Llama 3.1 70B Q4_K_M',  70.0,     4,    4096,   80,     8,    128),
]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    hdr = f'{"Model":<28} {"ctx":>6} {"Weights":>10} {"KV Cache":>10} {"Total":>10}'
    print(hdr)
    print('-' * len(hdr))
    for (name, pb, bits, ctx, lyr, kv_h, h_dim) in CONFIGS:
        r = estimate_vram(pb, bits, ctx, lyr, kv_h, h_dim)
        print(
            f'{name:<28} {ctx:>6,} '
            f'{r["weights_gb"]:>9.2f}G '
            f'{r["kv_cache_gb"]:>9.2f}G '
            f'{r["total_gb"]:>9.2f}G'
        )

# Expected output:
# Model                           ctx    Weights   KV Cache      Total
# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Phi-3 mini  Q4_K_M            4,096      2.04       0.38       2.42G
# Llama 3.1 8B Q4_K_M           4,096      4.33       0.52       4.85G
# Llama 3.1 8B Q4 32K          32,768      4.33       4.19       8.52G
# Llama 3.1 8B FP16             4,096     17.32       0.52      17.84G
# Mistral 7B Q5_K_M             8,192      5.13       1.05       6.18G
# Llama 3.1 70B Q4_K_M          4,096     37.86       1.56      39.42G
This script sends a long prompt to a locally running Ollama server and measures actual tokens per second, giving you empirical data about your specific hardware:

# bench_local.py
# Measure real tokens/sec from a local Ollama model.
# Requires: ollama serve is running with at least one model loaded
# Usage:    python bench_local.py --model llama3.1:8b

import argparse, json, time, urllib.request

PROMPT = (
    'Explain in detail how transformer attention mechanisms work: describe the query, '
    'key, and value projection matrices, the scaled dot-product attention formula, '
    'multi-head attention, and why positional encoding is necessary. '
    'Include the intuition behind each design decision.'
)

def run_bench(model, host='http://localhost:11434'):
    url = f'{host}/api/generate'
    payload = json.dumps({'model': model, 'prompt': PROMPT, 'stream': True}).encode()
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        url, data=payload, headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'}
    )

    tokens = 0
    ttft = None
    t0 = time.perf_counter()

    with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=300) as resp:
        for raw in resp:
            chunk = raw.decode().strip()
            if not chunk:
                continue
            data = json.loads(chunk)
            if data.get('done'):
                break
            tokens += 1
            if ttft is None:
                ttft = time.perf_counter()

    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    return {
        'model': model,
        'tokens': tokens,
        'elapsed_sec': round(elapsed, 2),
        'tokens_per_sec': round(tokens / elapsed, 1),
        'time_to_first_token_ms': round((ttft - t0) * 1000, 1) if ttft else None,
    }


if __name__ == '__main__':
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument('--model', default='llama3.1:8b')
    args = ap.parse_args()

    print(f'Benchmarking: {args.model}')
    result = run_bench(args.model)

    print()
    for k, v in result.items():
        print(f'  {k:<30}: {v}')

# Typical output on RTX 4090 with llama3.1:8b Q4_K_M:
#   model                         : llama3.1:8b
#   tokens                        : 387
#   elapsed_sec                   : 5.4
#   tokens_per_sec                : 71.7
#   time_to_first_token_ms        : 118.4
Real-World Example — Private Contract Processing Pipeline

A legal technology company needs to extract structured data from confidential contracts: party names, effective and expiration dates, governing law, and key obligations. Their data handling policy prohibits sending contract text to external APIs. They must process 500 documents per day, with each document averaging 6,000 tokens, and the results must be JSON-structured for downstream database ingestion.

Hardware selected: a workstation with an NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM), 64 GB DDR5 system RAM, and a 2 TB NVMe SSD. Model selected: Mistral-7B-Instruct Q5_K_M, which occupies approximately 5.4 GB of VRAM and leaves 18 GB of headroom for the KV cache across the 8,192-token context window used for full-document processing. At 45 to 55 tokens per second output rate, each document completes in approximately 25 to 35 seconds. 500 documents per day therefore requires 3.5 to 5 hours of GPU time, comfortably within a single working day on this hardware.

# contract_pipeline.py
# Production contract extraction using local Ollama inference.
# Setup: ollama pull mistral:7b-instruct-q5_K_M
# Usage: python contract_pipeline.py

import json
from pathlib import Path
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(base_url='http://localhost:11434/v1', api_key='ollama')

SYSTEM = (
    'You are a legal extraction assistant. '
    'Extract structured data from the contract text. '
    'Respond only with valid JSON. Use null for any field not found in the text.'
)

SCHEMA = {
    'parties': ['party name strings'],
    'effective_date': 'YYYY-MM-DD or null',
    'expiration_date': 'YYYY-MM-DD or null',
    'governing_law': 'jurisdiction string or null',
    'key_obligations': ['obligation summary strings, maximum 5'],
}


def extract(contract_text):
    user_msg = (
        f'Extract these fields:\n\nSchema:\n{json.dumps(SCHEMA, indent=2)}'
        f'\n\nContract (first 5500 chars):\n{contract_text[:5500]}'
    )
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model='mistral:7b-instruct-q5_K_M',
        messages=[
            {'role': 'system', 'content': SYSTEM},
            {'role': 'user', 'content': user_msg},
        ],
        temperature=0.0,
        max_tokens=800,
        response_format={'type': 'json_object'},
    )
    return json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)


def process_folder(folder):
    results = []
    for path in sorted(Path(folder).glob('*.txt')):
        try:
            data = extract(path.read_text(encoding='utf-8'))
            results.append({'file': path.name, 'status': 'ok', 'data': data})
        except Exception as exc:
            results.append({'file': path.name, 'status': 'error', 'error': str(exc)})
        print(f'Processed: {path.name}')
    return results


if __name__ == '__main__':
    folder = Path('./contracts')
    folder.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

    # Create a sample for testing
    sample = (
        'SERVICE AGREEMENT\n'
        'Effective Date: March 1, 2025\n'
        'Parties: Acme Corp ("Client") and Widget LLC ("Vendor")\n'
        'Term: Expires February 28, 2027\n'
        'Governing Law: State of New York\n'
        'Vendor shall deliver 100 software licenses per month.\n'
        'Client shall pay invoices within 30 days of receipt.\n'
    )
    (folder / 'sample.txt').write_text(sample)

    output = process_folder(folder)
    print(json.dumps(output, indent=2))

# At 50 t/s: 6000-token doc -> ~25 sec/doc
# 500 docs/day -> 3.5 hours GPU time on RTX 4090 with Mistral 7B Q5_K_M
Mistake 1 — Selecting a Model Larger Than Available VRAM

The most common mistake is choosing a model based on benchmark rankings without first checking whether it fits in VRAM. The result is either a runtime failure or silent CPU offloading that makes the model impractically slow:

# Wrong: loading a 70B model on a machine with 8 GB VRAM
# Ollama will offload ~85% of layers to CPU RAM
# Expected speed: 1-3 tokens/sec — not interactive
ollama run llama3.1:70b

# Right: match the model to your VRAM budget
# 8 GB VRAM -> 7B or 8B Q4 model fits fully, runs at 35-55 t/s
ollama run llama3.1:8b

# To confirm GPU layer count in llama.cpp directly:
# ./llama-server -m model.gguf --n-gpu-layers 99 --verbose 2>&1 | grep 'offloaded'
# Look for: 'llm_load_tensors: offloaded 32/32 layers to GPU'
# If the number before the slash is less than total, you have CPU offloading
Mistake 2 — Insisting on Full Precision When Quantization Suffices

New users sometimes insist on FP32 or FP16 weights, assuming that higher numerical precision always produces better outputs. For conversational and instruction-following tasks, Q4_K_M provides near-identical quality at one-quarter the VRAM cost:

# VRAM cost comparison for a 7B model at different precision levels:
#
#   FP32  : 7B * 4 bytes * 1.15 overhead = ~28 GB  <- exceeds all consumer GPUs
#   FP16  : 7B * 2 bytes * 1.15 overhead = ~17 GB  <- needs RTX 4090 for a 7B model!
#   Q8_0  : 7B * 1 byte  * 1.15 overhead = ~9 GB
#   Q5_K_M: 7B * 0.625b  * 1.15 overhead = ~5.5 GB
#   Q4_K_M: 7B * 0.5 byte * 1.15 overhead = ~4.7 GB <- fits in a 6 GB GPU
#   Q2_K  : 7B * 0.25b   * 1.15 overhead = ~2.5 GB
#
# Quality comparison on MMLU (higher = better):
#   Llama 3.1 8B FP16:    65.2%   <- baseline
#   Llama 3.1 8B Q5_K_M:  64.9%   <- 0.3% lower; imperceptible in practice
#   Llama 3.1 8B Q4_K_M:  64.5%   <- 0.7% lower; rarely noticeable
#   Llama 3.1 8B Q2_K:    61.1%   <- 4.1% lower; noticeable on knowledge tasks
#
# Conclusion: start with Q4_K_M. Move to Q5_K_M if you have VRAM headroom
# and observe quality issues. Only use FP16/FP32 when precision is critical.
Mistake 3 — Setting Maximum Context Length Without Budgeting the KV Cache
A model that fits comfortably in VRAM at 4K context can overflow VRAM at 32K or 128K context due to KV cache growth. This is one of the most surprising OOM failures developers encounter:

# kv_cache_budget.py
# Calculate KV cache cost at various context lengths for Llama 3.1 8B.

def kv_gb(n_layers, n_kv_heads, head_dim, ctx_len, kv_precision_bytes=2):
    return (2 * n_layers * n_kv_heads * head_dim * ctx_len * kv_precision_bytes) / (1024**3)

MODEL_WEIGHTS_GB = 4.7   # Llama 3.1 8B Q4_K_M

print(f'{"Context Length":>16} {"KV Cache GB":>12} {"Total GB":>10} {"RTX 3080 (10GB)":>17} {"RTX 4090 (24GB)":>17}')
print('-' * 76)

for ctx in [4_096, 8_192, 16_384, 32_768, 65_536, 131_072]:
    kv = kv_gb(32, 8, 128, ctx)   # Llama 3.1 8B: 32 layers, 8 GQA heads, 128 head_dim
    total = MODEL_WEIGHTS_GB + kv
    rtx3080 = 'OK' if total < 9.5 else 'OOM RISK'
    rtx4090 = 'OK' if total < 23.0 else 'OOM RISK'
    print(f'{ctx:>16,} {kv:>12.2f} {total:>10.2f} {rtx3080:>17} {rtx4090:>17}')

# Output:
#   Context Length   KV Cache GB   Total GB   RTX 3080 (10GB)   RTX 4090 (24GB)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
#            4,096          0.52       5.22              OK                OK
#            8,192          1.05       5.75              OK                OK
#           16,384          2.10       6.80              OK                OK
#           32,768          4.19       8.89              OK                OK
#           65,536          8.39      13.09        OOM RISK                OK
#          131,072         16.78      21.48        OOM RISK                OK

# Lesson: for 128K context on the RTX 4090, you are within 2.5 GB of the VRAM limit.
# Any additional overhead (runtime buffers, other GPU processes) can push you over.
# Set OLLAMA_NUM_CTX to a value calculated to leave at least 1-2 GB of headroom.
and establish your VRAM ceiling before looking at any model repository. This prevents hours wasted on downloads that will not work.

For most instruction-following and conversational workloads, Q4_K_M provides 90 to 98 percent of FP16 quality at 25 percent of the VRAM cost. Only move to Q5_K_M or higher when you have measured a quality deficiency in your specific use case.
Reserve VRAM headroom for the KV cache:

Never size a model to consume 100 percent of available VRAM. Budget at least 20 percent as free space for the KV cache, especially when using long contexts or expecting to run multiple concurrent inference sessions.
NVMe sequential read speeds of 3,000 to 7,000 MB/s dramatically reduce model load time versus SATA SSD or HDD. For 40 GB models, the difference can be 5 to 20 minutes of startup time per session.
Verify GPU acceleration is active after installation:

After installing a runtime, send a short prompt and measure tokens per second. A 7B model on a modern consumer GPU should produce at least 40 tokens per second. If you see 2 to 6 tokens per second, you are on CPU — check GPU driver installation.
LLM inference saturates VRAM before CPU. Use
to watch both VRAM utilization and GPU compute utilization. High compute utilization during prefill and steady VRAM near model size during decode is the healthy pattern.
Calculate context length budget before setting it:
Use the KV cache formula before enabling long-context modes. Setting
without calculating whether the KV cache fits in your remaining VRAM will cause OOM crashes on the first long document.
Use Ollama's curated model library as a starting point:
Ollama's library shows VRAM requirements for each model variant and automatically selects quantization appropriate for detected hardware, reducing the risk of downloading files that will not load.
LLM inference has two distinct computational phases with different bottleneck profiles. The
processes the entire input prompt in a single batched forward pass. Because all input tokens are available simultaneously, the GPU processes them in parallel and the workload is
— tensor cores are fully active, and higher TFLOPS improve prefill speed. A 10,000-token document can be prefilled in 2 to 5 seconds on a consumer GPU.
generates output tokens one at a time. Each new token requires a full forward pass through every transformer layer. This sequential, single-token-per-pass workload is

— the GPU's tensor cores are underutilized while waiting for VRAM reads. This is why two GPUs with the same memory bandwidth produce similar decode throughput even if one has twice the TFLOPS. For interactive chat, decode speed is the bottleneck that matters most.

For models that exceed a single GPU's VRAM, tensor parallelism splits weight matrices across multiple GPUs. Each GPU holds one horizontal shard of every weight matrix and computes a partial result, which is then reduced across GPUs to form the full output. NVIDIA NVLink provides 600 to 900 GB/s of inter-GPU bandwidth, enabling nearly linear throughput scaling across NVLink-connected GPUs. Multi-GPU inference over PCIe alone, at only 16 GB/s per direction, suffers from synchronization bottlenecks and does not scale linearly — you might achieve 1.5x to 1.8x throughput from two PCIe-connected GPUs where NVLink-connected GPUs would give 1.9x to 2.0x.

Speculative decoding is an inference optimization where a small, fast draft model proposes multiple candidate tokens, and the larger target model verifies all of them in a single batched forward pass. When draft tokens are accepted (which happens roughly 70 to 80 percent of the time for a well-matched draft/target pair), you gain multiple output tokens for the cost of one target-model pass. This technique can increase effective decode throughput by 1.5 to 3 times without changing model quality. The trade-off is that both models must reside in VRAM simultaneously, so speculative decoding requires substantially more VRAM than running the target model alone.

Apple's M-series chips use a unified memory architecture: CPU and GPU share the same physical DRAM pool at full memory bandwidth. An M3 Ultra with 192 GB of unified memory running at 800 GB/s can hold a 70B model in Q4 quantization (approximately 40 GB) with ample KV cache headroom, something that requires two 40 GB NVIDIA A100s on conventional hardware. The cost advantage is significant: a Mac Studio M2 Ultra with 192 GB costs roughly 6,000 USD versus 30,000 USD or more for two A100 40GB cards. The trade-off is throughput: Metal is slower than CUDA for matrix operations, and Apple Silicon produces roughly 15 to 25 tokens per second for a 70B model, versus 60 to 100 tokens per second on a dual-A100 system. For workloads where quality matters and speed is secondary, Apple Silicon at the high memory tier is the most cost-effective local option.

Beginner-friendly local inference stack with a curated model library, hardware auto-detection, automatic GGUF quantization selection, and an OpenAI-compatible API at localhost:11434. Covers most single-user development use cases.

The C++ inference engine underlying Ollama. Provides granular control over GPU layer count, batch size, context length, rope scaling, and quantization format. Use when you need configuration flexibility beyond Ollama's defaults, or when embedding inference directly into a C/C++ application.

A production-grade Python inference server implementing PagedAttention for efficient KV cache management, continuous batching across multiple concurrent users, and tensor parallelism across multiple GPUs. Requires CUDA and PyTorch. The standard choice for serving multiple users from a shared GPU server.

A desktop GUI application for macOS, Windows, and Linux backed by llama.cpp. Provides a chat interface, model browser, and local API server. The easiest path for non-developers who want to run local models.

A structured generation runtime optimized for complex tool use, structured JSON output, and multi-turn agentic workflows. Often faster than vLLM on workloads with constrained output formats because it exploits output structure to optimize the decode graph.

# nvitop — interactive real-time GPU monitoring (strongly recommended)
pip install nvitop
nvitop

# Standard NVIDIA monitoring
nvidia-smi                           # one-shot snapshot
watch -n 1 nvidia-smi               # refresh every second
nvidia-smi dmon -s u                # continuous utilization stream

# Track VRAM specifically
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=memory.used,memory.free,memory.total,temperature.gpu \
           --format=csv,noheader -l 2

# Apple Silicon monitoring
pip install asitop
sudo asitop

# System RAM during inference (useful for CPU offload scenarios)
watch -n 1 free -h
Shows the VRAM footprint for every model variant in the Ollama library, filterable by size. The fastest way to identify models that fit your hardware.
Include hardware requirements, recommended quantization levels, and benchmark results for the vast majority of publicly released models.
The most thorough and frequently updated consumer GPU comparison for deep learning, with price-to-bandwidth analysis relevant to LLM inference.
Community-submitted tokens-per-second benchmarks across hardware configurations and model sizes, updated regularly.
Comparison — Hardware Options for Local LLM Inference
Apple M2 Ultra / M3 Ultra (192 GB)
Mental Model — The Bookshelf and Desk Analogy

Think of running a local LLM like using a very large reference encyclopedia. The encyclopedia's pages are the model's weight parameters — billions of them. VRAM is your desk: only what physically fits on the desk can be read at full speed. System RAM is a nearby bookshelf: you can reach it, but retrieving a volume takes a few extra seconds. The NVMe SSD is a storage room down the hall: loading the encyclopedia at startup takes time, but once it is on your desk you do not go back to the storage room during a conversation.

Tokens per second corresponds to how quickly you can flip through pages and transcribe passages. If the entire encyclopedia fits on your desk (the model is fully in VRAM), you work at desk speed — fast and uninterrupted. If you must keep walking to the bookshelf mid-sentence (CPU layer offloading), each lookup is slow regardless of how quickly you read once you have the right page. Quantization is like getting a concise edition of the same encyclopedia — smaller pages, slightly condensed detail, but the same topics covered adequately for most questions you would practically ask.

The KV cache is the notepad on your desk: as a conversation grows longer, you jot down key facts from earlier turns so you do not have to re-read the entire encyclopedia. The notepad grows with each exchange. If the notepad and the encyclopedia together overflow your desk (VRAM), older notepad pages fall off — this is context truncation. That is why a model handling a very long document on a memory-constrained GPU may appear to forget earlier content. The desk has simply run out of space.

The most common reliability failure in local LLM deployments is a CUDA out-of-memory error. When VRAM is exhausted, the CUDA runtime raises an unrecoverable exception that kills the inference process — unlike a clean API error response, this typically crashes the entire server and requires a restart. The most effective prevention strategies are: leaving at least 10 to 20 percent of VRAM free at model load time, setting an explicit context length limit that accounts for the KV cache budget, using runtimes with dynamic KV cache management such as vLLM's PagedAttention, and monitoring VRAM usage in production with alerting when usage exceeds 85 percent of capacity.

Running models locally means downloading multi-gigabyte binary files from public model repositories. A maliciously crafted GGUF file could theoretically exploit parser vulnerabilities in the loading library. Best practices to mitigate this risk: only download models from verified sources such as official model authors, Ollama's curated library, or established organizations on HuggingFace; verify SHA256 hashes when the model card provides them; run inference processes in Docker containers or VMs with filesystem access limited to the model directory; and never run the inference server process as root or administrator.

Ollama, vLLM, and llama.cpp all expose unauthenticated HTTP APIs by default. Any process on your machine can send requests to the local inference server, including browser extensions and other software running in the same user session. On a shared machine or when serving over a LAN, this is a meaningful attack surface: a prompt injection via a malicious webpage or API call could interact with any documents or context the model has access to. Mitigations include binding the server to 127.0.0.1 rather than 0.0.0.0, adding an API key requirement in front of the inference server via a reverse proxy for any networked deployment, and not giving the inference process filesystem access beyond what it needs to load the model.

LLM inference runs GPUs at sustained 100 percent memory and compute utilization — a workload profile that consumer cooling systems were not designed to handle for extended periods. Monitor GPU temperature with

and ensure sustained temperatures remain below the thermal throttle threshold, which is typically 83 to 87 degrees Celsius for consumer NVIDIA cards. Temperatures above this threshold cause automatic clock reduction, which reduces inference speed by 10 to 30 percent. For production inference servers intended to run 24 hours a day, either use data center GPUs designed for continuous load (A100, H100), or implement GPU undervolting on consumer cards to reduce heat generation at modest performance cost.
VRAM is the non-negotiable constraint for local inference:

GPU video memory cannot be upgraded after purchase. Know your VRAM ceiling before selecting any model. A model that overflows VRAM will either fail to load or run 10 to 30 times slower through CPU offloading.
Estimate VRAM requirements with a formula before downloading:
gigabytes. Add the KV cache cost
for your target context length. Total this before downloading any file.
Q4_K_M is the correct default for most use cases:

Four-bit GGUF quantization provides 90 to 98 percent of FP16 quality at 25 percent of the VRAM cost. Only upgrade to Q5 or Q8 quantization when you can measure a specific quality deficiency in your actual task.
Memory bandwidth governs decode throughput, not TFLOPS:

LLM token generation is memory-bandwidth bound. The RTX 4090's 1008 GB/s bandwidth is the primary reason it produces 65 to 95 tokens per second on 7B models. When evaluating GPU hardware for inference specifically, bandwidth per dollar matters more than raw compute throughput.
The KV cache grows with context length and consumes real VRAM:

A model that fits at 4K token context may overflow VRAM at 32K or 128K. Always calculate your KV cache budget alongside your weight budget, and leave at least 20 percent of VRAM free at model load time.
Ollama is the fastest path to a working local inference setup:

It abstracts model download, quantization selection, GPU detection, and API exposure into a single tool. Use it for development and single-user deployments; graduate to vLLM or llama.cpp when you need production-grade serving or lower-level control.
Apple Silicon offers the highest VRAM capacity at consumer price points:

M2 Ultra and M3 Ultra with up to 192 GB of unified memory can run models that require multiple enterprise GPUs on NVIDIA hardware, at significantly lower cost, though with lower raw decode throughput per token.
Local inference APIs have no authentication by default:

Always bind local servers to 127.0.0.1 for development use, and add API key authentication via a reverse proxy for any networked deployment. Treat the model API as a trust boundary equivalent to a database endpoint.
Validate your setup empirically before committing to production workloads:

Measure actual tokens per second with a benchmark script after setup. If you see 2 to 6 tokens per second on a 7B model, you are on CPU; if you see 40 to 100, you are on GPU. Never assume GPU acceleration is active without measuring it.
